text:
Code refactoring means cleaning up your code without changing what it does. The goal is to improve the structure, making it easier to read and understand. For example, you might rename unclear variables, break long functions into smaller ones, or delete duplicate code. The program's behavior stays exactly the same, but the code becomes simpler and more organized.

This practice is important for managing technical debt, which builds up when developers take shortcuts to save time. Over time, messy code becomes difficult to modify and debug. Regular refactoring reduces this debt, helping teams work faster and reduce mistakes. In short, it keeps the code healthy and the project sustainable.
